act_tone: More confident, Tony starts sharing personal stories, JARVIS recovers more
jarvis_state: Recovering — can hold brief conversations, run basic diagnostics

mission.narrative: Display real-time environmental data on the OLED screen.

sections.text: Ah there you are Explorer welcome to day 18 of our fantastic Interstellar Adventure I hope you're well rested and ready to embark on today's Journey great work on the last mission Sherlock you've got the brains but now going to need the brawn your keypad worked fine for Real Time input into the hero but we'll need precise thrust control for our Ascent rate to the surface without risking any let's say unplanned rapid disassembly or we could run straight into Rachel and same thing game over fortunately the hero kit contains just the sensor we need for subtle or forceful control a rotary encoder all right let's dive into today's Hardware discussion our star of the day is the rotary encoder but what exactly is it in simple terms a rotary encoder sends direction of rotation signals and the amount of turn it can also send an on or off signal with a push pick it up and play with it every click you feel as you turn the encoder is counted and the direction you're turning it is noted these two pieces of information are sent to our board as a sequence of high high and low signals the rotary encoder is particularly useful for scrolling through menus adjusting variables on the Fly and in our case delicately controlling our Ascent rate moving on it's time for some nerdy distinctions there are two types of encoders absolute and relative an absolute encoder has a unique value for every specific angular position of the knob there's a maximum clockwise position and a maximum counterclockwise position you know precisely where you are at all times a relative encoder on the other hand like the one we're using today can turn endlessly it doesn't have an end so to speak it tells you how far and in which direction the knob has been turned from its starting position but it doesn't give you a specific location our relative encoder has 20 distinct clicks for a complete 360° rotation that means each click corresponds to an 18° rotation that's our encoder's angular resolution now that we have a good understanding of our new component let's talk about how we're going to hook it up to our control panel we connect the 5V to the plus symbol or VCC terminal and ground to the gnd D terminal CLK or a and DT or B are the clever bits they help us figure out which way the knob is being turned there's also an S SW terminal that's for the push switch built into the encoder push down on the knob and it acts like a button neat huh but we won't be using that feature in our project today I think it's time we move on to the code wouldn't you agree right let's get a bit technical now this rotating encoder Gadget is all about timing we want to know two things one the direction of rotation and two the rate of rotation the direction of the rotation is rather straightforward when the knob starts turning one of our two signals CLK or a and DT or B will get excited and go high before the other which one goes first tells us the direction of rotation essentially if our CLK signal gets all giddy and turns high before our GT signal we know the rotary encoder is spinning clockwise like the hands on a traditional clock if on the other hand our DT signal jumps the gun and goes high before CLK we know the rotary encoder is spinning counterclockwise isn't that just a slice of fried gold the rate of rotation is a bit more more complex I think we'll save that juicy bit for later for now let's make sure we get that Direction detection working perfectly right so we know that the order in which our signals go high determines the direction of rotation but how do we accurately and efficiently detect that enter our secret weapon the interrupt an interrupt is a rather brilliant feature in microcontrollers that says Hey listen something important just happened stop what you're doing and pay attention to this right now this allows us to interrupt the normal flow of our code to react to a specific event in our case when either CLK or DT goes from high to low or from low to high or in simple words when either CLK or DT change their state instead of continually asking are We There Yet to CLK or DT in a loop which is very tiring mind you we set an interrupt on CLK and DT and then go about our business as soon as one of them changes state the interrupt fires and our code can react immediately to handle the event once the interrupt executes the code will resume exactly where it left for the interrupt it's like setting an alarm to wake you up at 7:00 a.m. Instead of waking up every few minutes to check the time it's efficient isn't it that's the magic of interrupts for you Explorer right today we've got two primary tasks to tackle encode task one we need to read the direction our rotary encoder is spinning which we'll manage by using a counter of sorts consider it our trusty breadcrumb chil to keep track of where we've been test two we have to ensure our spacecraft doesn't leap like a giddy frog from altitude 0 to 100 stick with caution sudden Rises could rip holes in the hull from rocks rise too slow and there might not be enough Force to get unstuck from Ocean mud constant Rises could run you into Rachel the giant whike creature hovering around the ship we need to maintain a steady controlled ascent and that means putting safeguards in our code these are specific stopping points at precise altitudes we'll use our seven segment display as our handy altitude meter and adjust our altitude with the rotary encoder we're not competing in a high-speed race so there's no rush remember the Fable of the celestial Tortoise and the comet hair that's right we're taking a page from the tortoise's book slow and steady wins the race adjust the altitude check the display and ensure the steady Ascent of our craft one step at a time my dear Intrepid Explorer woo if I had a human voice like you I think I'd have lost it by now I think it's time we turn to some instructional material that I've gathered cached on the computer I've also added it to your uh well you know the drill hello and welcome to day 18 before we start did you get the numbers from yesterday did did you write them down do you have them because if you don't today will not work for you so make sure that you have the codes from yesterday and if they aren't working please be sure to check that make sure that you shifted with numbers included but if you shift with just the alphabet and not characters 0-9 it will not work so make sure once you have those numbers you'll have this down here which is the keys that are needed to unlock your control system if you don't have all three of those keys you will need to replace this with the first this is the second and this with the third key so make sure you do that otherwise your code will not work it will not compile in order to reduce any risk of unauthorized use that is why there is such a high security on the controls new Concepts that we'll be talking about today when it comes to coding are interrupts which allows the currently executing code to be well interrupted to handle some event and then return to the executing code as though nothing had happened you can think of it as like in the middle of me talking someone ta what's up okay and then coming back to the speech like nothing happened that's my way of inter interpreting it I guess internally although it's maybe a strange example down here we have another function which is going to allow us to use those interrupts which just simply says attach interrupt we're essentially going to attach an interrupt on an event or when something happens it's going to trigger the interrupt to occur then we have digital pin to interrupt which converts a PIN number to an internal hero interrupt pin please note the HERO board only supports interrupts on pins two and three and the other pin will not work for interrupts new parts that were introduced in this lesson include the rotary encoder which allows a sketch to respond to rotational input clockwise or counterclockwise and can also provide a button input when pressed down and since I'm using the Arduino IDE for this example I don't need that I'm going to remove it but if you're not using the Arduino IDE let's say you're on some other simulator out there maybe WWE or using visual studio you will need it next we're going to include the seven we can continue on forward now I don't want you to get confused with the seven segment display pins and the rotary encoder pins they both have clock pins and they both have this is a digital input output and this has a DT pin they look very similar at a glance this is going to be defining the pins for our rotary encoder and this is going to be defining the pins for our s segment display try not to get them confused they do look very similar though the rot re encoder is used for the depth controller and then the seven segment display will be our depth gauge displaying what depth we are at now here we have the basic encoder object just like the 4x4 keypad Library where we need to create an object just like the 7 segment display Library like here where we had to create an object we need to create an object for our library so that we can call the code we're just going to create we're going to call this one depth control for our rotary encoder because it's quite literally going to be our setup today is a little bit more complicated than what you might have been used to for setups yes we're doing a little bit more today Believe It or Not than just setting our serial Monitor and some pinouts we have a little bit more because we are introducing the concept of interrupts we're going to start the serial Monitor and then we're going to set the brightness to the maximum brightness on times what our current depth is on and off to the user and let's display it and blink it however many times that we defined up above again we said three if you want to change it up there you don't have to change it in a million places hopping back to setup if the keys are valid then we want to show our initial depth otherwise we're going to see a big old nope on our seven segment display because guess what your keys weren't valid and then we're going to print to the serial monitor saying error invalid keys because you did not get the correct keys if you don't see this and it gets past this point then that means your key should hopefully be valed and there's nothing to worry about down below past this point we have the following interrupts the New Concept for the day the new fun stuff now assuming you're using pins to or three we're going to use the digital pin to interrupt clock pin and DT pin which again if as you can see is quite literally what we put the rotary encoder on pins two and three is what's required for this to work so the rotary encoder does not work well unless you use interrupts there are other ways that this can be implemented but it's going to work very poorly without using interrupts in your code I could go into a lot of reasons why that is but just you can just trust me on this there are a lot of bugs if you don't use an interrupt there are going to be two different interrupts that we are going to be defining again this is in setup this happens beforehand it needs three things in each one one is the pin that we're going to be using is a digital pin to interrupt we're going to set depth control clock pin in this case and in the second one the DT pin as the one that's going to be it's going to be watching throughout the entire code to check to see when it what changes that's the third macro change if it's a change on here if anything changes it's going to then do something what is it going to do it's going to call a function and that function is something we defined down below called update encoder so any time this changes we're going to update the encoder or we're going to call the update encoder function which simply just does this depth control. Service the service function handles all the calculations that track turning off the dial and update the counter which we'll be reading inside of our Loop since we don't want to reinvent the wheel let's let the library do it for us after those are all defined we can hop down to Loop now this first if statement says the following if depth control doget change get change checks the previous value when going through the loop this time going through the loop if the value is different if let's just say you went from -60 and you went up to 59 in this time of like and then by this time of the loop because the interrupt happened it's at a different value that would turn this to true meaning will going to go and execute the rest of this code if there hasn't been any changes we don't need to execute anything down here below so you can just ignore it for right now but everything for the most part is all inside of this if statement so only if something changed do we want to do anything int current depth is going to be equal to our initial depth which is -60 plus depth control.

Getet count so what this means that the very first time we go through the loop we're going to have a get count of zero because we didn't turn it at all next time we go with the RP we're going to have for you getting out of the water and with that that should be all of the code explanation for day 18 I wish you good luck tomorrow on the travels back up to the ocean surface welcome to the day 18 wiring example video it looks like you were successful in decrypting the codes needed to access today's module and are ready to move forward to the next step at least I hope you were or this might turn out to be a short session today we'll learn to use a a new control device a rotary encoder like this one when connected to the hero it can provide information about the direction and amount of rotation applied to it with an angular resolution of one10 of a rotation we'll be using the encoder here to simulate the control of the ascent of our vehicle while using the four-digit display that we learned about last time to monitor our progress now let's have a look at the connections of the encoder and how we've Incorporated it into our system we see that it has five pins although we'll only be using four of those today just like the display unit the two bottom pins right here are for ground and plus 5 Vols and they're also connected up here by the traditional black and red wires that we usually use for those functions you'll notice that we've set up a ground bus and a 5vt bus over here on our on our breadboard to be shared by the encoder and by the display so that we'll only need a single connection to the hero to provide ground and VCC to both of these devices so here you can see is our 5V connection to the hero and this black wire provides the ground down to the ground bus on on our breadboard a quick note in passing here you can see that we've chosen to use some fairly long wires to provide the connections to both the display and the encoder and although theoretically we could H perhaps choose to plug these components directly in to the breadboard but this would Orient those parts at an awkward right angle rendering them difficult to see and difficult to use so for the sake of user friendliness we've opted here to Mount them horizontally on my custom work platform and wire them up this way and so you should feel free to use your own Ingenuity to mount these components in a way that's convenient for you pause the video at this point if you want to take a minute to study this setup and satisfy yourself that it's equivalent to what's shown in the wiring the direction and amount of rotation consistent with our software sketch we've connected these two upper wires to pins two and three respectively now the middle pin on our encoder here is labeled s SW for switch and it's used to detect button presses on the encoder shaft so if you press on the shaft of the encoder it's a push button now we're not using that feature in today's project so on our circuit we've left that pin this purple wire here unconnected because it's not being used today now as before the display will be using pins six and five for its CLK and Dio functions so as far as the
